title: America First Equipment and Information Act
short_title: America First Equipment and Information Act
plain_summary: This law says the United States cannot give any military equipment, weapons technology, or secret information to Russia. The President must write a report to Congress every year to show that the government is following this rule. This helps make sure America keeps its military secrets safe and does not help Russia with defense materials.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the America First Equipment and Information Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these important facts. First, the Russian Federation has taken hostile actions that put United States national security and world stability at risk. Second, the United States must make sure that no military equipment, technology, or secret government information falls into the hands of enemies who could use it against the United States or our allies. Third, stopping the sale, loan, or trade of United States military equipment and secret information to Russia helps protect our national security.

sections.title: Section 3  Prohibition on Certain Assistance to Russia and Related Actions with Respect to Russia
sections.text: Starting on the day this law goes into effect, these actions are not allowed. The United States cannot provide Foreign Military Financing to Russia (this means government money to help buy military equipment). The United States cannot provide Foreign Military Sales to Russia (this means direct government sales of military equipment). The United States cannot provide Direct Commercial Sales to Russia (this means allowing private companies to sell military equipment). The President cannot use drawdown authority to help Russia (this means the President cannot quickly send military supplies from existing stockpiles). The government cannot remove Russia from any rules in the International Traffic in Arms Regulation (these are rules that control military equipment exports). The Bureau of Industry and Security of the Department of Commerce cannot remove any export controls on Russia (these are rules that limit what products can be sold to Russia). The United States cannot share any information or intelligence with Russia.

sections.title: Section 4  Enforcement
sections.text: The President must send a report every year to the appropriate congressional committees. This report must explain how well the United States is following this law, including details about any military support to Russia. The appropriate congressional committees are specific groups in Congress that oversee these issues. In the House of Representatives, these are the Committee on Foreign Affairs, the Committee on Armed Services, and the Committee on the Judiciary. In the Senate, these are the Committee on Foreign Relations, the Committee on Armed Services, and the Committee on the Judiciary.
